revision.comment: 日本神話に登場する草の神
revision.text:
'''カヤノヒメ'''（'''カヤヌヒメ'''とも）は、[[日本神話]]に登場する[[草]]の[[神 (神道)|神]]である。[[古事記]]では'''鹿屋野比売神'''、[[日本書紀]]では'''草祖草野姫'''（くさのおやかやのひめ。草祖は草の祖神の意味）と表記し、古事記では別名が'''野椎神'''（のづちのかみ）であると記している。
[[神産み]]において[[イザナギ]]・[[イザナミ]]の間に生まれた。古事記においては、山の神である[[オオヤマツミ]]との間に、4対8柱の神を生んだ。神名の「カヤ」は[[萱]]のことである。萱は屋根を葺くのに使われるなど、人間にとって身近な草であり、草の代表として草の神の名前となった。
樽前山神社（[[北海道]][[苫小牧市]]）では山の神・大山津見神、木の神・[[ククノチ|久々能智神]]と共に祀られている。萱津神社（愛知県海部市）では日本唯一の漬物の神として祀られており、[[タバコ]]の葉の生産地ではタバコの神として信仰されている。
別名の「ノヅチ」は「野の精霊（野つ霊）」の意味である。しかし、この「ノヅチ」は後に[[マムシ]]の別名とされるようになり、さらには[[蛇]]の化け物とされるようになった。ノヅチは、その名前から、頭と尻は同じ大きさで柄のない槌の形をしているとされ、人の足に食いつくとされた。[[ツチノコ]]も蛇の化け物としてのノヅチから派生したものである。
==関連項目== *[[日本の神の一覧]]
{{stub}}

==関連項目== *[[日本の神の一覧]]
[[Category:日本の神|かやのひめ]] [[Category:草木の神|かやのひめ]]

[[Image:Creation myths of Japan 3.svg|thumb|right|250px|神産み神話（イザナキ・イザナミが生んだ神々） [[media:Creation myths of Japan 3.svg|SVGで表示（対応ブラウザのみ）]]]] '''カヤノヒメ'''（'''カヤヌヒメ'''とも）は、[[日本神話]]に登場する[[草]]の[[神 (神道)|神]]である。[[古事記]]では'''鹿屋野比売神'''、[[日本書紀]]では'''草祖草野姫'''（くさのおやかやのひめ。草祖は草の祖神の意味）と表記し、古事記では別名が'''野椎神'''（のづちのかみ）であると記している。
[[神産み]]において[[イザナギ]]・[[イザナミ]]の間に生まれた。古事記においては、山の神である[[オオヤマツミ]]との間に、4対8柱の神を生んだ。神名の「カヤ」は[[萱]]のことである。萱は屋根を葺くのに使われるなど、人間にとって身近な草であり、草の代表として草の神の名前となった。
[[樽前山神社]]（[[北海道]][[苫小牧市]]）では山の神・大山津見神、木の神・[[ククノチ|久々能智神]]と共に祀られている。萱津神社（[[愛知県]][[海部郡 (愛知県)|海部郡]][[甚目寺町]]）では日本唯一の漬物の神として祀られており、[[タバコ]]の葉の生産地ではタバコの神として信仰されている。

[[ファイル:Creation myths of Japan 3.svg|thumb|right|250px|神産み神話（イザナギ・イザナミが生んだ神々） [[media:Creation myths of Japan 3.svg|SVGで表示（対応ブラウザのみ）]]]] '''カヤノヒメ'''（'''カヤヌヒメ'''とも）は、[[日本神話]]に登場する[[草]]の[[神 (神道)|神]]である。『[[古事記]]』では'''鹿屋野比売神'''、『[[日本書紀]]』では'''草祖草野姫'''（くさのおやかやのひめ。草祖は草の祖神の意味）と表記し、『古事記』では別名が'''野椎神'''（のづちのかみ）であると記している。
[[神産み]]において[[イザナギ]]・[[イザナミ]]の間に生まれた。『古事記』においては、山の神である[[オオヤマツミ]]との間に、4対8柱の神を生んだ。神名の「カヤ」は[[カヤ (草)|萱]]のことである。萱は屋根を葺くのに使われるなど、人間にとって身近な草であり、草の代表として草の神の名前となった。
[[樽前山神社]]（[[北海道]][[苫小牧市]]）では山の神・大山津見神、木の神・[[ククノチ|久々能智神]]と共に祀られている。[[萱津神社]]（[[愛知県]][[海部郡 (愛知県)|海部郡]][[甚目寺町]]）では日本唯一の漬物の神として祀られており、[[タバコ]]の葉の生産地ではタバコの神として信仰されている。
別名の「'''ノヅチ'''（'''[[野槌]]'''）」は「野の精霊（野つ霊）」の意味である。しかし、この「ノヅチ」は後に[[ニホンマムシ]]の別名とされるようになり、さらには[[ヘビ|蛇]]の化け物とされるようになった。野槌は、その名前から、頭と尻は同じ大きさで柄のない槌の形をしているとされ、人の足に食いつくとされた。[[ツチノコ]]も蛇の化け物としての野槌から派生したものである。

[[神産み]]において[[伊弉諾尊]] （いざなぎ）・[[伊弉冉尊 ]]（いざなみ）の間に生まれた。 『古事記』においては、[[山]]の神である[[オオヤマツミ|大山祇神]]との間に、4対8柱の神を生んだ。神名の「カヤ」は[[カヤ (草)|萱]]のことである。
萱は屋根を葺くのに使われるなど、人間にとって身近な草であり、家の屋根の葺く草の霊として草の神の名前となった。
別名の「'''ノヅチ'''（'''[[野槌]]'''）」は「野の精霊（野つ霊）」の意味である。
==祀られる神社== [[樽前山神社]]（[[北海道]][[苫小牧市]]）では山の神・大山祇神（おおややまつみ）、木の神・[[ククノチ|句句廼馳]]（くくのち）と共に祀られている。
[[萱津神社]]（[[愛知県]][[あま市]]）では日本唯一の[[漬物]]の神として祀られており、[[タバコ]]の葉の生産地では煙草の神として信仰されている。
==関連項目== *[[日本の神の一覧]] *[[神産み]]
{{神道 横}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:かやのひめ}} [[Category:日本の神]] [[Category:天津神]] [[Category:草木の神]] [[Category:女神]]
[[es:Kaya-no-hime]]

[[ファイル:Creation myths of Japan 3.svg|thumb|right|250px|神産み神話（イザナギ・イザナミが生んだ神々） [[media:Creation myths of Japan 3.svg|SVGで表示（対応ブラウザのみ）]]]] '''カヤノヒメ'''（'''カヤヌヒメ'''とも）は、[[日本神話]]に登場する[[草本|草]]の[[神 (神道)|神]]である。

[[神産み]]において[[イザナギ|伊弉諾尊]] （いざなぎ）・[[イザナミ|伊弉冉尊]]（いざなみ）の間に生まれた。 『古事記』においては、[[山]]の神である[[オオヤマツミ|大山祇神]]との間に、4対8柱の神を生んだ。神名の「カヤ」は[[カヤ (草)|萱]]のことである。
萱は屋根を葺くのに使われるなど、人間にとって身近な草であり、家の屋根の葺く草の霊として草の神の名前となった。
別名の「'''ノヅチ'''（'''[[野槌]]'''）」は「野の精霊（野つ霊）」の意味である。
== 祀られる神社 == [[樽前山神社]]（[[北海道]][[苫小牧市]]）では山の神・大山祇神（おおややまつみ）、木の神・[[ククノチ|句句廼馳]]（くくのち）と共に祀られている。
[[萱津神社]]（[[愛知県]][[あま市]]）では日本唯一の[[漬物]]の神として祀られており、[[タバコ]]の葉の生産地では煙草の神として信仰されている。

[[ファイル:Creation myths of Japan 3.svg|thumb|right|250px|神産み神話（イザナギ・イザナミが生んだ神々） [[media:Creation myths of Japan 3.svg|SVGで表示（対応ブラウザのみ）]]]] '''カヤノヒメ'''は、[[日本神話]]に登場する[[草本|草]]の[[神 (神道)|神]]である。 『[[古事記]]』では'''鹿屋野比売神'''、『[[日本書紀]]』では'''草祖草野姫'''（くさのおやかやのひめ。草祖は草の祖神の意味）と表記し、『古事記』では別名が'''野椎神'''（のづちのかみ）であると記している。
== 神話での記述 == [[神産み]]において[[伊邪那岐命]] （いざなぎ）・[[伊邪那美命]]（いざなみ）の間に生まれた。 『古事記』においては、[[山]]の神である[[大山津見神]]との間に、4対8柱の神を生んだ。
== 神名 == 神名の「カヤ」は[[カヤ (草)|萱]]のことである。
萱は屋根を葺くのに使われるなど、人間にとって身近な草であり、家の屋根の葺く草の霊として草の神の名前となった。
別名の「'''ノヅチ'''（'''[[野槌]]'''）」は「野の精霊（野つ霊）」の意味である。
== 祀られる神社 == [[樽前山神社]]（[[北海道]][[苫小牧市]]）では山の神・大山祇神（おおややまつみ）、木の神・[[ククノチ|句句廼馳]]（くくのち）と共に祀られている。
[[萱津神社]]（[[愛知県]][[あま市]]）では日本唯一の[[漬物]]の神として祀られている。
[[清野井庭神社]]（[[三重県]][[伊勢市]]）では[[用水路|灌漑用水]]の神、別説では屋船の神の[[分霊]]であるという<ref>伊勢文化舎（2008）：52 - 53ページ</ref>。
== 脚注 == {{Reflist}}
== 参考文献 == * 『お伊勢さん125社めぐり』別冊『伊勢人』、[[伊勢文化舎]]、平成20年12月23日、151p. ISBN 978-4-900759-37-4
== 関連項目 == * [[日本の神の一覧]] * [[神産み]]
{{日本神話}} {{神道 横}} {{Shinto-stub}}
{{DEFAULTSORT:かやのひめ}} [[Category:日本の女神]] [[Category:国津神]] [[Category:草木の神]]

revision.text:
{{Short description|Shinto kami}} {{Expand language|topic=|langcode=ja|otherarticle=カヤノヒメ|date=October 2022}} {{Page numbers needed|date=August 2021}} {{Infobox deity | type = Japanese | name = Kaya-no-hime | deity_of = Goddess of vegetation, grass and fields | member_of = | image = | caption = | other_names = {{Nihongo|Kanoyanohime-no-Kami|鹿屋野比売神|}}, {{Nihongo|Kusanooyakaya-no-hime|草祖草野姫|}}, {{Nihongo|Kusano-hime|草野姫命|}}, and {{Nihongo|Nozuchi|野槌|}} | script_name = Japanese | script = 草祖草野 | parents = [[Izanami]] and [[Izanagi]] | siblings = [[Ōyamatsumi]] | offspring = | gender = Female }}
{{Nihongo|'''Kaya-no-hime'''|草祖草野|}}, also called {{Nihongo|'''Kanoyanohime-no-Kami'''|鹿屋野比売神|}}, {{Nihongo|'''Kusanooyakaya-no-hime'''|草祖草野姫|}}, {{Nihongo|'''Kusano-hime'''|草野姫命|}}, or {{Nihongo|'''Nozuchi'''|野槌|}} (meaning Moorland Elder),<ref>{{Cite web|title=Kayanohime • A History of Japan - 日本歴史|url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/kayanohime/|access-date=2020-10-14|website=A History of Japan - 日本歴史|language=en-GB}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&q=Nozuchi-no-kami&pg=PA501|title=Japan Encyclopedia|author=Louis-Frédéric|date=2005|publisher=Harvard University Press|isbn=978-0-674-01753-5|language=en}}</ref> is the Japanese goddess of vegetation, grass, and fields. She is considered protector of fields.<ref> {{Cite book|last1=Picken|first1=Stuart D. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=yA3_QqC6pPgC&dq=Kaya-no-hime&pg=PA99|title=Essentials of Shinto: An Analytical Guide to Principal Teachings|last2=Picken|first2=Stuart|date=1994|publisher=Greenwood Publishing Group|isbn=978-0-313-26431-3|language=en}} </ref><ref>{{Cite book|last1=Buttimer|first1=Anne|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=zUHFyGQcJxgC&q=Nozuchi-no-kami&pg=PA51|title=Nature and Identity in Cross-Cultural Perspective|last2=Wallin|first2=L.|date=1999-07-31|publisher=Springer Science & Business Media|isbn=978-0-7923-5651-6|language=en}}</ref> She is also considered the ancestor of [[herb]]s.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Aveni|first=Anthony|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=ueolEAAAQBAJ&dq=Kaya-no-hime&pg=PA144|title=Creation Stories: Landscapes and the Human Imagination|date=2021-04-20|publisher=Yale University Press|isbn=978-0-300-25124-1|pages=144|language=en|author-link=Anthony Aveni}}</ref>
She is the daughter of [[Izanami]] and [[Izanagi]]. She is married to her husband and brother [[Ōyamatsumi]], and gave birth to eight deities. Some versions of her origin story claim she is also the mother of [[Konohanasakuya-hime]].<ref> {{Cite book|last1=Coulter|first1=Charles Russell|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=VWxekbhM1yEC&dq=Kaya-no-hime&pg=PT604|title=Encyclopedia of Ancient Deities|last2=Turner|first2=Patricia|date=2013-07-04|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-135-96397-2|language=en}} </ref>
== Worship == In the past, people often prayed to her before cutting down wood or reeds for building.<ref>{{Cite book|last1=Hastings|first1=James|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=nZ5DAQAAMAAJ&q=Kaya-no-hime&pg=PA467|title=Encyclopaedia of Religion and Ethics: Sacrifice-Sudra|last2=Selbie|first2=John Alexander|date=1921|publisher=T. & T. Clark|isbn=978-0-567-06509-4|language=en}}</ref> She was worshipped because the plants that she favored provided the raw materials for furniture and houses.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Aston|first=W. G.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=FT6yDwAAQBAJ&dq=Kaya-no-hime&pg=PA120|title=Shinto|date=2019-09-25|publisher=BoD – Books on Demand|isbn=978-3-7340-7989-4|pages=120|language=en}}</ref>

{{Short description|Shinto kami}} {{Expand language|topic=|langcode=ja|otherarticle=カヤノヒメ|date=October 2022}} {{Page numbers needed|date=August 2021}} {{Infobox deity | type = Japanese | name = Kaya-no-hime | deity_of = Goddess of vegetation, grass and fields | member_of = | image = | caption = | other_names = {{Nihongo|Kanoyanohime-no-Kami|鹿屋野比売神|}}, {{Nihongo|Kusanooyakaya-no-hime|草祖草野姫|}}, {{Nihongo|Kusano-hime|草野姫命|}}, and {{Nihongo|Nozuchi|野槌|}} | script_name = Japanese | script = 草祖草野 | parents = [[Izanami]] and [[Izanagi]] | siblings = [[Ōyamatsumi]] | offspring = | gender = Female }}
{{Nihongo|'''Kaya-no-hime'''|草祖草野|}}, also called {{Nihongo|'''Kanoyanohime-no-Kami'''|鹿屋野比売神|}}, {{Nihongo|'''Kusanooyakaya-no-hime'''|草祖草野姫|}}, {{Nihongo|'''Kusano-hime'''|草野姫命|}}, or {{Nihongo|'''Nozuchi'''|野槌|}} (meaning Moorland Elder),<ref>{{Cite web|title=Kayanohime • A History of Japan - 日本歴史|url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/kayanohime/|access-date=2020-10-14|website=A History of Japan - 日本歴史|language=en-GB}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&q=Nozuchi-no-kami&pg=PA501|title=Japan Encyclopedia|author=Louis-Frédéric|date=2005|publisher=Harvard University Press|isbn=978-0-674-01753-5|language=en}}</ref> is the Japanese goddess of vegetation, grass, and fields. She is considered protector of fields.<ref> {{Cite book|last1=Picken|first1=Stuart D. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=yA3_QqC6pPgC&dq=Kaya-no-hime&pg=PA99|title=Essentials of Shinto: An Analytical Guide to Principal Teachings|last2=Picken|first2=Stuart|date=1994|publisher=Greenwood Publishing Group|isbn=978-0-313-26431-3|language=en}} </ref><ref>{{Cite book|last1=Buttimer|first1=Anne|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=zUHFyGQcJxgC&q=Nozuchi-no-kami&pg=PA51|title=Nature and Identity in Cross-Cultural Perspective|last2=Wallin|first2=L.|date=1999-07-31|publisher=Springer Science & Business Media|isbn=978-0-7923-5651-6|language=en}}</ref> She is also considered the ancestor of [[herb]]s.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Aveni|first=Anthony|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=ueolEAAAQBAJ&dq=Kaya-no-hime&pg=PA144|title=Creation Stories: Landscapes and the Human Imagination|date=2021-04-20|publisher=Yale University Press|isbn=978-0-300-25124-1|pages=144|language=en|author-link=Anthony Aveni}}</ref>
She is the daughter of [[Izanami]] and [[Izanagi]]. She is married to her husband and brother [[Ōyamatsumi]], and gave birth to eight deities. Some versions of her origin story claim she is also the mother of [[Konohanasakuya-hime]].<ref> {{Cite book|last1=Coulter|first1=Charles Russell|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=VWxekbhM1yEC&dq=Kaya-no-hime&pg=PT604|title=Encyclopedia of Ancient Deities|last2=Turner|first2=Patricia|date=2013-07-04|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-135-96397-2|language=en}} </ref>
== Worship == In the past, people often prayed to her before cutting down wood or reeds for building.<ref>{{Cite book|last1=Hastings|first1=James|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=nZ5DAQAAMAAJ&q=Kaya-no-hime&pg=PA467|title=Encyclopaedia of Religion and Ethics: Sacrifice-Sudra|last2=Selbie|first2=John Alexander|date=1921|publisher=T. & T. Clark|isbn=978-0-567-06509-4|language=en}}</ref> She was worshipped because the plants that she favored provided the raw materials for furniture and houses.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Aston|first=W. G.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=FT6yDwAAQBAJ&dq=Kaya-no-hime&pg=PA120|title=Shinto|date=2019-09-25|publisher=BoD – Books on Demand|isbn=978-3-7340-7989-4|pages=120|language=en}}</ref>

Clark|isbn=978-0-567-06509-4|language=en}}</ref> She was worshipped because the plants that she favored provided the raw materials for furniture and houses.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Aston|first=W. G.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=FT6yDwAAQBAJ&dq=Kaya-no-hime&pg=PA120|title=Shinto|date=2019-09-25|publisher=BoD – Books on Demand|isbn=978-3-7340-7989-4|pages=120|language=en}}</ref>

{{Short description|Shinto kami}} {{Expand language|topic=|langcode=ja|otherarticle=カヤノヒメ|date=October 2022}} {{Page numbers needed|date=August 2021}} {{Infobox deity | type = Japanese | name = Kaya-no-hime | deity_of = Goddess of vegetation, grass and fields | member_of = | image = | caption = | other_names = {{Nihongo|Kanoyanohime-no-Kami|鹿屋野比売神|}}, {{Nihongo|Kusanooyakaya-no-hime|草祖草野姫|}}, {{Nihongo|Kusano-hime|草野姫命|}}, and {{Nihongo|Nozuchi|野槌|}} | script_name = Japanese | script = 草祖草野 | parents = [[Izanami]] and [[Izanagi]] | siblings = [[Ōyamatsumi]] | offspring = | gender = Female }}
{{Nihongo|'''Kaya-no-hime'''|草祖草野|}}, also called {{Nihongo|'''Kanoyanohime-no-Kami'''|鹿屋野比売神|}}, {{Nihongo|'''Kusanooyakaya-no-hime'''|草祖草野姫|}}, {{Nihongo|'''Kusano-hime'''|草野姫命|}}, or {{Nihongo|'''Nozuchi'''|野槌|}} (meaning Moorland Elder),<ref>{{Cite web|title=Kayanohime • A History of Japan - 日本歴史|url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/kayanohime/|access-date=2020-10-14|website=A History of Japan - 日本歴史|language=en-GB}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&q=Nozuchi-no-kami&pg=PA501|title=Japan Encyclopedia|author=Louis-Frédéric|date=2005|publisher=Harvard University Press|isbn=978-0-674-01753-5|language=en}}</ref> is the Japanese goddess of vegetation, grass, and fields. She is considered protector of fields.<ref> {{Cite book|last1=Picken|first1=Stuart D. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=yA3_QqC6pPgC&dq=Kaya-no-hime&pg=PA99|title=Essentials of Shinto: An Analytical Guide to Principal Teachings|last2=Picken|first2=Stuart|date=1994|publisher=Greenwood Publishing Group|isbn=978-0-313-26431-3|language=en}} </ref><ref>{{Cite book|last1=Buttimer|first1=Anne|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=zUHFyGQcJxgC&q=Nozuchi-no-kami&pg=PA51|title=Nature and Identity in Cross-Cultural Perspective|last2=Wallin|first2=L.|date=1999-07-31|publisher=Springer Science & Business Media|isbn=978-0-7923-5651-6|language=en}}</ref> She is also considered the ancestor of [[herb]]s.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Aveni|first=Anthony|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=ueolEAAAQBAJ&dq=Kaya-no-hime&pg=PA144|title=Creation Stories: Landscapes and the Human Imagination|date=2021-04-20|publisher=Yale University Press|isbn=978-0-300-25124-1|pages=144|language=en|author-link=Anthony Aveni}}</ref>
She is the daughter of [[Izanami]] and [[Izanagi]]. She is married to her husband and brother [[Ōyamatsumi]], and gave birth to eight deities. Some versions of her origin story claim she is also the mother of [[Konohanasakuya-hime]].<ref> {{Cite book|last1=Coulter|first1=Charles Russell|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=VWxekbhM1yEC&dq=Kaya-no-hime&pg=PT604|title=Encyclopedia of Ancient Deities|last2=Turner|first2=Patricia|date=2013-07-04|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-135-96397-2|language=en}} </ref>
== Worship == In the past, people often prayed to her before cutting down wood or reeds for building.<ref>{{Cite book|last1=Hastings|first1=James|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=nZ5DAQAAMAAJ&q=Kaya-no-hime&pg=PA467|title=Encyclopaedia of Religion and Ethics: Sacrifice-Sudra|last2=Selbie|first2=John Alexander|date=1921|publisher=T. & T. Clark|isbn=978-0-567-06509-4|language=en}}</ref> She was worshipped because the plants that she favored provided the raw materials for furniture and houses.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Aston|first=W. G.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=FT6yDwAAQBAJ&dq=Kaya-no-hime&pg=PA120|title=Shinto|date=2019-09-25|publisher=BoD – Books on Demand|isbn=978-3-7340-7989-4|pages=120|language=en}}</ref>

{{Short description|Shinto kami}} {{Expand language|topic=|langcode=ja|otherarticle=カヤノヒメ|date=October 2022}} {{Page numbers needed|date=August 2021}} {{Infobox deity | type = Japanese | name = Kaya-no-hime | deity_of = Goddess of vegetation, grass and fields | member_of = | image = | caption = | other_names = {{Nihongo|Kanoyanohime-no-Kami|鹿屋野比売神|}}, {{Nihongo|Kusanooyakaya-no-hime|草祖草野姫|}}, {{Nihongo|Kusano-hime|草野姫命|}}, and {{Nihongo|Nozuchi|野槌|}} | script_name = Japanese | script = 草祖草野 | parents = [[Izanami]] and [[Izanagi]] | siblings = [[Ōyamatsumi]] | offspring = | gender = Female }}
{{Nihongo|'''Kaya-no-hime'''|草祖草野|}}, also called {{Nihongo|'''Kanoyanohime-no-Kami'''|鹿屋野比売神|}}, {{Nihongo|'''Kusanooyakaya-no-hime'''|草祖草野姫|}}, {{Nihongo|'''Kusano-hime'''|草野姫命|}}, or {{Nihongo|'''Nozuchi'''|野槌|}} (meaning Moorland Elder),<ref>{{Cite web|title=Kayanohime • A History of Japan - 日本歴史|url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/kayanohime/|access-date=2020-10-14|website=A History of Japan - 日本歴史|language=en-GB}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&q=Nozuchi-no-kami&pg=PA501|title=Japan Encyclopedia|author=Louis-Frédéric|date=2005|publisher=Harvard University Press|isbn=978-0-674-01753-5|language=en}}</ref> is the Japanese goddess of vegetation, grass, and fields. She is considered protector of fields.<ref> {{Cite book|last1=Picken|first1=Stuart D. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=yA3_QqC6pPgC&dq=Kaya-no-hime&pg=PA99|title=Essentials of Shinto: An Analytical Guide to Principal Teachings|last2=Picken|first2=Stuart|date=1994|publisher=Greenwood Publishing Group|isbn=978-0-313-26431-3|language=en}} </ref><ref>{{Cite book|last1=Buttimer|first1=Anne|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=zUHFyGQcJxgC&q=Nozuchi-no-kami&pg=PA51|title=Nature and Identity in Cross-Cultural Perspective|last2=Wallin|first2=L.|date=1999-07-31|publisher=Springer Science & Business Media|isbn=978-0-7923-5651-6|language=en}}</ref> She is also considered the ancestor of [[herb]]s.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Aveni|first=Anthony|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=ueolEAAAQBAJ&dq=Kaya-no-hime&pg=PA144|title=Creation Stories: Landscapes and the Human Imagination|date=2021-04-20|publisher=Yale University Press|isbn=978-0-300-25124-1|pages=144|language=en|author-link=Anthony Aveni}}</ref>
She is the daughter of [[Izanami]] and [[Izanagi]]. She is married to her husband and brother [[Ōyamatsumi]], and gave birth to eight deities. Some versions of her origin story claim she is also the mother of [[Konohanasakuya-hime]].<ref> {{Cite book|last1=Coulter|first1=Charles Russell|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=VWxekbhM1yEC&dq=Kaya-no-hime&pg=PT604|title=Encyclopedia of Ancient Deities|last2=Turner|first2=Patricia|date=2013-07-04|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-135-96397-2|language=en}} </ref>
== Worship == In the past, people often prayed to her before cutting down wood or reeds for building.<ref>{{Cite book|last1=Hastings|first1=James|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=nZ5DAQAAMAAJ&q=Kaya-no-hime&pg=PA467|title=Encyclopaedia of Religion and Ethics: Sacrifice-Sudra|last2=Selbie|first2=John Alexander|date=1921|publisher=T. & T. Clark|isbn=978-0-567-06509-4|language=en}}</ref> She was worshipped because the plants that she favored provided the raw materials for furniture and houses.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Aston|first=W. G.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=FT6yDwAAQBAJ&dq=Kaya-no-hime&pg=PA120|title=Shinto|date=2019-09-25|publisher=BoD – Books on Demand|isbn=978-3-7340-7989-4|pages=120|language=en}}</ref>

{{Short description|Shinto kami}} {{Expand language|topic=|langcode=ja|otherarticle=カヤノヒメ|date=October 2022}} {{Page numbers needed|date=August 2021}} {{Infobox deity | type = Japanese | name = Kaya-no-hime | deity_of = Goddess of vegetation, grass and fields | member_of = | image = | caption = | other_names = {{Nihongo|Kanoyanohime-no-Kami|鹿屋野比売神|}}, {{Nihongo|Kusanooyakaya-no-hime|草祖草野姫|}}, {{Nihongo|Kusano-hime|草野姫命|}}, and {{Nihongo|Nozuchi|野槌|}} | script_name = Japanese | script = 草祖草野 | parents = [[Izanami]] and [[Izanagi]] | siblings = [[Ōyamatsumi]] | offspring = | gender = Female }}
{{Nihongo|'''Kaya-no-hime'''|草祖草野|}}, also called {{Nihongo|'''Kanoyanohime-no-Kami'''|鹿屋野比売神|}}, {{Nihongo|'''Kusanooyakaya-no-hime'''|草祖草野姫|}}, {{Nihongo|'''Kusano-hime'''|草野姫命|}}, or {{Nihongo|'''Nozuchi'''|野槌|}} (meaning Moorland Elder),<ref>{{Cite web|title=Kayanohime • A History of Japan - 日本歴史|url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/kayanohime/|access-date=2020-10-14|website=A History of Japan - 日本歴史|language=en-GB}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&q=Nozuchi-no-kami&pg=PA501|title=Japan Encyclopedia|author=Louis-Frédéric|date=2005|publisher=Harvard University Press|isbn=978-0-674-01753-5|language=en}}</ref> is the Japanese goddess of vegetation, grass, and fields. She is considered protector of fields.<ref> {{Cite book|last1=Picken|first1=Stuart D. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=yA3_11C6pPgC&dq=Kaya-no-hime&pg=PA99|title=Essentials of Shinto: An Analytical Guide to Principal Teachings|last2=Picken|first2=Stuart|date=1994|publisher=Greenwood Publishing Group|isbn=978-0-313-26431-3|language=en}} </ref><ref>{{Cite book|last1=Buttimer|first1=Anne|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=zUHFyGQcJxgC&q=Nozuchi-no-kami&pg=PA51|title=Nature and Identity in Cross-Cultural Perspective|last2=Wallin|first2=L.|date=1999-07-31|publisher=Springer Science & Business Media|isbn=978-0-7923-5651-6|language=en}}</ref> She is also considered the ancestor of [[herb]]s.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Aveni|first=Anthony|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=ueolEAAAQBAJ&dq=Kaya-no-hime&pg=PA144|title=Creation Stories: Landscapes and the Human Imagination|date=2021-04-20|publisher=Yale University Press|isbn=978-0-300-25124-1|pages=144|language=en|author-link=Anthony Aveni}}</ref>
She is the daughter of [[Izanami]] and [[Izanagi]]. She is married to her husband and brother [[Ōyamatsumi]], and gave birth to eight deities. Some versions of her origin story claim she is also the mother of [[Konohanasakuya-hime]].<ref> {{Cite book|last1=Coulter|first1=Charles Russell|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=VWxekbhM1yEC&dq=Kaya-no-hime&pg=PT604|title=Encyclopedia of Ancient Deities|last2=Turner|first2=Patricia|date=2013-07-04|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-135-96397-2|language=en}} </ref>
== Worship == In the past, people often prayed to her before cutting down wood or reeds for building.<ref>{{Cite book|last1=Hastings|first1=James|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=nZ5DAQAAMAAJ&q=Kaya-no-hime&pg=PA467|title=Encyclopaedia of Religion and Ethics: Sacrifice-Sudra|last2=Selbie|first2=John Alexander|date=1921|publisher=T. & T. Clark|isbn=978-0-567-06509-4|language=en}}</ref> She was worshipped because the plants that she favored provided the raw materials for furniture and houses.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Aston|first=W. G.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=FT6yDwAAQBAJ&dq=Kaya-no-hime&pg=PA120|title=Shinto|date=2019-09-25|publisher=BoD – Books on Demand|isbn=978-3-7340-7989-4|pages=120|language=en}}</ref>

{{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Japanese goddesses]] [[Category:Kunitsukami]] [[Category:Kaya-no-hime]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:redirects]] [[Category:bbb]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:refreshing redirects 1-26]] [[Category:refreshing redirects]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:カヤノヒメ]] [[Category:Shrine stubs]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 1]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] [[Category:pages which might possibly be incomplete but are most likely complete]] [[Category:I think I cleared all the incomplete or blank pages earlier]] }} [[en:Kaya-no-hime]] [[en:Kaya]] [[sn:Kaya-no-hime]] [[ja:カヤノヒメ]] [[sn:aya-no-hime]]

{{Short description|Shinto kami}} {{Expand language|topic=|langcode=ja|otherarticle=カヤノヒメ|date=October 2022}} {{Page numbers needed|date=August 2021}} {{Infobox deity | type = Japanese | name = Kaya-no-hime | deity_of = Goddess of vegetation, grass and fields | member_of = | image = | caption = | other_names = {{Nihongo|Kanoyanohime-no-Kami|鹿屋野比売神|}}, {{Nihongo|Kusanooyakaya-no-hime|草祖草野姫|}}, {{Nihongo|Kusano-hime|草野姫命|}}, and {{Nihongo|Nozuchi|野槌|}} | script_name = Japanese | script = 草祖草野 | parents = [[Izanami]] and [[Izanagi]] | siblings = [[Ōyamatsumi]] | offspring = | gender = Female }}
{{Nihongo|'''Kaya-no-hime'''|草祖草野|}}, also called {{Nihongo|'''Kanoyanohime-no-Kami'''|鹿屋野比売神|}}, {{Nihongo|'''Kusanooyakaya-no-hime'''|草祖草野姫|}}, {{Nihongo|'''Kusano-hime'''|草野姫命|}}, or {{Nihongo|'''Nozuchi'''|野槌|}} (meaning Moorland Elder),<ref>{{Cite web|title=Kayanohime • A History of Japan - 日本歴史|url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/kayanohime/|access-date=2020-10-14|website=A History of Japan - 日本歴史|language=en-GB}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&q=Nozuchi-no-kami&pg=PA501|title=Japan Encyclopedia|author=Louis-Frédéric|date=2005|publisher=Harvard University Press|isbn=978-0-674-01753-5|language=en}}</ref> is the Japanese goddess of vegetation, grass, and fields. She is considered protector of fields.<ref> {{Cite book|last1=Picken|first1=Stuart D. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=yA3_11C6pPgC&dq=Kaya-no-hime&pg=PA99|title=Essentials of Shinto: An Analytical Guide to Principal Teachings|last2=Picken|first2=Stuart|date=1994|publisher=Greenwood Publishing Group|isbn=978-0-313-26431-3|language=en}} </ref><ref>{{Cite book|last1=Buttimer|first1=Anne|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=zUHFyGQcJxgC&q=Nozuchi-no-kami&pg=PA51|title=Nature and Identity in Cross-Cultural Perspective|last2=Wallin|first2=L.|date=1999-07-31|publisher=Springer Science & Business Media|isbn=978-0-7923-5651-6|language=en}}</ref> She is also considered the ancestor of [[herb]]s.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Aveni|first=Anthony|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=ueolEAAAQBAJ&dq=Kaya-no-hime&pg=PA144|title=Creation Stories: Landscapes and the Human Imagination|date=2021-04-20|publisher=Yale University Press|isbn=978-0-300-25124-1|pages=144|language=en|author-link=Anthony Aveni}}</ref>
She is the daughter of [[Izanami]] and [[Izanagi]]. She is married to her husband and brother [[Ōyamatsumi]], and gave birth to eight deities. Some versions of her origin story claim she is also the mother of [[Konohanasakuya-hime]].<ref> {{Cite book|last1=Coulter|first1=Charles Russell|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=VWxekbhM1yEC&dq=Kaya-no-hime&pg=PT604|title=Encyclopedia of Ancient Deities|last2=Turner|first2=Patricia|date=2013-07-04|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-135-96397-2|language=en}} </ref>
== Worship == In the past, people often prayed to her before cutting down wood or reeds for building.<ref>{{Cite book|last1=Hastings|first1=James|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=nZ5DAQAAMAAJ&q=Kaya-no-hime&pg=PA467|title=Encyclopaedia of Religion and Ethics: Sacrifice-Sudra|last2=Selbie|first2=John Alexander|date=1921|publisher=T. & T. Clark|isbn=978-0-567-06509-4|language=en}}</ref> She was worshipped because the plants that she favored provided the raw materials for furniture and houses.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Aston|first=W. G.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=FT6yDwAAQBAJ&dq=Kaya-no-hime&pg=PA120|title=Shinto|date=2019-09-25|publisher=BoD – Books on Demand|isbn=978-3-7340-7989-4|pages=120|language=en}}</ref>
== References == {{reflist}} {{Japan-myth-stub}}

{{Short description|Shinto kami}} {{Expand language|topic=|langcode=ja|otherarticle=カヤノヒメ|date=October 2022}} {{Page numbers needed|date=August 2021}} {{Infobox deity | type = Japanese | name = Kaya-no-hime | deity_of = Goddess of vegetation, grass and fields | member_of = | image = | caption = | other_names = {{Nihongo|Kanoyanohime-no-Kami|鹿屋野比売神|}}, {{Nihongo|Kusanooyakaya-no-hime|草祖草野姫|}}, {{Nihongo|Kusano-hime|草野姫命|}}, and {{Nihongo|Nozuchi|野槌|}} | script_name = Japanese | script = 草祖草野 | parents = [[Izanami]] and [[Izanagi]] | siblings = [[Ōyamatsumi]] | offspring = | gender = Female }}
{{Nihongo|'''Kaya-no-hime'''|草祖草野|}}, also called {{Nihongo|'''Kanoyanohime-no-Kami'''|鹿屋野比売神|}}, {{Nihongo|'''Kusanooyakaya-no-hime'''|草祖草野姫|}}, {{Nihongo|'''Kusano-hime'''|草野姫命|}}, or {{Nihongo|'''Nozuchi'''|野槌|}} (meaning Moorland Elder),<ref>{{Cite web|title=Kayanohime • A History of Japan - 日本歴史|url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/kayanohime/|access-date=2020-10-14|website=A History of Japan - 日本歴史|language=en-GB}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&q=Nozuchi-no-kami&pg=PA501|title=Japan Encyclopedia|author=Louis-Frédéric|date=2005|publisher=Harvard University Press|isbn=978-0-674-01753-5|language=en}}</ref> is the Japanese goddess of vegetation, grass, and fields. She is considered protector of fields.<ref> {{Cite book|last1=Picken|first1=Stuart D. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=yA3_11C6pPgC&dq=Kaya-no-hime&pg=PA99|title=Essentials of Shinto: An Analytical Guide to Principal Teachings|last2=Picken|first2=Stuart|date=1994|publisher=Greenwood Publishing Group|isbn=978-0-313-26431-3|language=en}} </ref><ref>{{Cite book|last1=Buttimer|first1=Anne|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=zUHFyGQcJxgC&q=Nozuchi-no-kami&pg=PA51|title=Nature and Identity in Cross-Cultural Perspective|last2=Wallin|first2=L.|date=1999-07-31|publisher=Springer Science & Business Media|isbn=978-0-7923-5651-6|language=en}}</ref> She is also considered the ancestor of [[herb]]s.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Aveni|first=Anthony|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=ueolEAAAQBAJ&dq=Kaya-no-hime&pg=PA144|title=Creation Stories: Landscapes and the Human Imagination|date=2021-04-20|publisher=Yale University Press|isbn=978-0-300-25124-1|pages=144|language=en|author-link=Anthony Aveni}}</ref>
She is the daughter of [[Izanami]] and [[Izanagi]]. She is married to her husband and brother [[Ōyamatsumi]], and gave birth to eight deities. Some versions of her origin story claim she is also the mother of [[Konohanasakuya-hime]].<ref> {{Cite book|last1=Coulter|first1=Charles Russell|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=VWxekbhM1yEC&dq=Kaya-no-hime&pg=PT604|title=Encyclopedia of Ancient Deities|last2=Turner|first2=Patricia|date=2013-07-04|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-135-96397-2|language=en}} </ref>
== Worship == In the past, people often prayed to her before cutting down wood or reeds for building.<ref>{{Cite book|last1=Hastings|first1=James|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=nZ5DAQAAMAAJ&q=Kaya-no-hime&pg=PA467|title=Encyclopaedia of Religion and Ethics: Sacrifice-Sudra|last2=Selbie|first2=John Alexander|date=1921|publisher=T. & T. Clark|isbn=978-0-567-06509-4|language=en}}</ref> She was worshipped because the plants that she favored provided the raw materials for furniture and houses.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Aston|first=W. G.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=FT6yDwAAQBAJ&dq=Kaya-no-hime&pg=PA120|title=Shinto|date=2019-09-25|publisher=BoD – Books on Demand|isbn=978-3-7340-7989-4|pages=120|language=en}}</ref>
== References == {{reflist}} {{Japan-myth-stub}}

{{Nihongo|'''Kaya-no-hime'''|草祖草野|}}, also called {{Nihongo|'''Kanoyanohime-no-Kami'''|鹿屋野比売神|}}, {{Nihongo|'''Kusanooyakaya-no-hime'''|草祖草野姫|}}, {{Nihongo|'''Kusano-hime'''|草野姫命|}}, or {{Nihongo|'''Nozuchi'''|野槌|}} (meaning Moorland Elder),<ref>{{Cite web|title=Kayanohime • A History of Japan - 日本歴史|url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/kayanohime/|access-date=2020-10-14|website=A History of Japan - 日本歴史|language=en-GB}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&q=Nozuchi-no-kami&pg=PA501|title=Japan Encyclopedia|author=Louis-Frédéric|date=2005|publisher=Harvard University Press|isbn=978-0-674-01753-5|language=en}}</ref> is the Japanese goddess of vegetation, grass, and fields. She is considered protector of fields.<ref> {{Cite book|last1=Picken|first1=Stuart D. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=yA3_11C6pPgC&dq=Kaya-no-hime&pg=PA99|title=Essentials of Shinto: An Analytical Guide to Principal Teachings|last2=Picken|first2=Stuart|date=1994|publisher=Greenwood Publishing Group|isbn=978-0-313-26431-3|language=en}} </ref><ref>{{Cite book|last1=Buttimer|first1=Anne|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=zUHFyGQcJxgC&q=Nozuchi-no-kami&pg=PA51|title=Nature and Identity in Cross-Cultural Perspective|last2=Wallin|first2=L.|date=1999-07-31|publisher=Springer Science & Business Media|isbn=978-0-7923-5651-6|language=en}}</ref> She is also considered the ancestor of [[herb]]s.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Aveni|first=Anthony|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=ueolEAAAQBAJ&dq=Kaya-no-hime&pg=PA144|title=Creation Stories: Landscapes and the Human Imagination|date=2021-04-20|publisher=Yale University Press|isbn=978-0-300-25124-1|pages=144|language=en|author-link=Anthony Aveni}}</ref>
She is the daughter of [[Izanami]] and [[Izanagi]]. She is married to her husband and brother [[Ōyamatsumi]], and gave birth to eight deities. Some versions of her origin story claim she is also the mother of [[Konohanasakuya-hime]].<ref> {{Cite book|last1=Coulter|first1=Charles Russell|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=VWxekbhM1yEC&dq=Kaya-no-hime&pg=PT604|title=Encyclopedia of Ancient Deities|last2=Turner|first2=Patricia|date=2013-07-04|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-135-96397-2|language=en}} </ref>
== Worship == In the past, people often prayed to her before cutting down wood or reeds for building.<ref>{{Cite book|last1=Hastings|first1=James|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=nZ5DAQAAMAAJ&q=Kaya-no-hime&pg=PA467|title=Encyclopaedia of Religion and Ethics: Sacrifice-Sudra|last2=Selbie|first2=John Alexander|date=1921|publisher=T. & T. Clark|isbn=978-0-567-06509-4|language=en}}</ref> She was worshipped because the plants that she favored provided the raw materials for furniture and houses.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Aston|first=W. G.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=FT6yDwAAQBAJ&dq=Kaya-no-hime&pg=PA120|title=Shinto|date=2019-09-25|publisher=BoD – Books on Demand|isbn=978-3-7340-7989-4|pages=120|language=en}}</ref>
== References == {{reflist}} {{Japan-myth-stub}}

revision.text:
[[ファイル:Descendants of Izanagi and Izanami.svg|thumb|right|250px|神産み神話（イザナギ・イザナミが生んだ神々） [[media:Descendants of Izanagi and Izanami.svg|SVGで表示（対応ブラウザのみ）]]]] '''カヤノヒメ'''は、[[日本神話]]に登場する[[草本|草]]の[[神 (神道)|神]]である。 『[[古事記]]』では'''鹿屋野比売神'''、『[[日本書紀]]』では'''草祖草野姫'''（くさのおやかやのひめ。草祖は草の祖神の意味）と表記し、『古事記』では別名が'''野椎神'''（のづちのかみ）であると記している。
== 神話での記述 == [[神産み]]において[[イザナギ|伊邪那岐命]] （いざなぎ）・[[イザナミ|伊邪那美命]]（いざなみ）の間に生まれた。 『古事記』においては、[[山]]の神である[[オオヤマツミ|大山津見神]]との間に、4対8柱の神を生んだ。
== 神名 == 神名の「カヤ」は[[カヤ (草)|萱]]のことである。
萱は屋根を葺くのに使われるなど、人間にとって身近な草であり、家の屋根の葺く草の霊として草の神の名前となった。
別名の「'''ノヅチ'''（'''[[野槌]]'''）」は「野の精霊（野つ霊）」の意味である。
== 祀られる神社 == [[樽前山神社]]（[[北海道]][[苫小牧市]]）では山の神・大山祇神（おおややまつみ）、木の神・[[ククノチ|句句廼馳]]（くくのち）と共に祀られている。
[[萱津神社]]（[[愛知県]][[あま市]]）では日本唯一の[[漬物]]の神として祀られている。
[[清野井庭神社]]（[[三重県]][[伊勢市]]）では[[用水路|灌漑用水]]の神、別説では屋船の神の[[分霊]]であるという<ref>伊勢文化舎（2008）：52 - 53ページ</ref>。
== 脚注 == {{Reflist}}
== 参考文献 == * 『お伊勢さん125社めぐり』別冊『伊勢人』、[[伊勢文化舎]]、平成20年12月23日、151p. ISBN 978-4-900759-37-4
== 関連項目 == * [[日本の神の一覧]] * [[神産み]]
{{日本神話}} {{神道 横}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:かやのひめ}} [[Category:日本の神]] [[Category:国津神]] [[Category:草木の神]] [[Category:女神]]

She is considered protector of fields.<ref> {{Cite book|last1=Picken|first1=Stuart D. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=yA3_11C6pPgC&dq=Kaya-no-hime&pg=PA99|title=Essentials of Shinto: An Analytical Guide to Principal Teachings|last2=Picken|first2=Stuart|date=1994|publisher=Greenwood Publishing Group|isbn=978-0-313-26431-3|language=en}} </ref><ref>{{Cite book|last1=Buttimer|first1=Anne|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=zUHFyGQcJxgC&q=Nozuchi-no-kami&pg=PA51|title=Nature and Identity in Cross-Cultural Perspective|last2=Wallin|first2=L.|date=1999-07-31|publisher=Springer Science & Business Media|isbn=978-0-7923-5651-6|language=en}}</ref> She is also considered the ancestor of [[herb]]s.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Aveni|first=Anthony|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=ueolEAAAQBAJ&dq=Kaya-no-hime&pg=PA144|title=Creation Stories: Landscapes and the Human Imagination|date=2021-04-20|publisher=Yale University Press|isbn=978-0-300-25124-1|pages=144|language=en|author-link=Anthony Aveni}}</ref>
She is the daughter of [[Izanami]] and [[Izanagi]]. She is married to her husband and brother [[Ōyamatsumi]], and gave birth to eight deities. Some versions of her origin story claim she is also the mother of [[Konohanasakuya-hime]].<ref> {{Cite book|last1=Coulter|first1=Charles Russell|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=VWxekbhM1yEC&dq=Kaya-no-hime&pg=PT604|title=Encyclopedia of Ancient Deities|last2=Turner|first2=Patricia|date=2013-07-04|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-135-96397-2|language=en}} </ref>
== Worship == In the past, people often prayed to her before cutting down wood or reeds for building.<ref>{{Cite book|last1=Hastings|first1=James|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=nZ5DAQAAMAAJ&q=Kaya-no-hime&pg=PA467|title=Encyclopaedia of Religion and Ethics: Sacrifice-Sudra|last2=Selbie|first2=John Alexander|date=1921|publisher=T. & T. Clark|isbn=978-0-567-06509-4|language=en}}</ref> She was worshipped because the plants that she favored provided the raw materials for furniture and houses.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Aston|first=W. G.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=FT6yDwAAQBAJ&dq=Kaya-no-hime&pg=PA120|title=Shinto|date=2019-09-25|publisher=BoD – Books on Demand|isbn=978-3-7340-7989-4|pages=120|language=en}}</ref>

== Worship == In the past, people often prayed to her before cutting down wood or reeds for building.<ref>{{Cite book|last1=Hastings|first1=James|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=nZ5DAQAAMAAJ&q=Kaya-no-hime&pg=PA467|title=Encyclopaedia of Religion and Ethics: Sacrifice-Sudra|last2=Selbie|first2=John Alexander|date=1921|publisher=T. & T. Clark|isbn=978-0-567-06509-4|language=en}}</ref> She was worshipped because the plants that she favored provided the raw materials for furniture and houses.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Aston|first=W. G.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=FT6yDwAAQBAJ&dq=Kaya-no-hime&pg=PA120|title=Shinto|date=2019-09-25|publisher=BoD – Books on Demand|isbn=978-3-7340-7989-4|pages=120|language=en}}</ref>

{{Short description|Shinto kami}} {{Expand language|topic=|langcode=ja|otherarticle=カヤノヒメ|date=October 2022}} {{Page numbers needed|date=August 2021}} {{Infobox deity | type = Japanese | name = Kaya-no-hime | deity_of = Goddess of vegetation, grass and fields | member_of = | image = | caption = | other_names = {{Nihongo|Kanoyanohime-no-Kami|鹿屋野比売神|}}, {{Nihongo|Kusanooyakaya-no-hime|草祖草野姫|}}, {{Nihongo|Kusano-hime|草野姫命|}}, and {{Nihongo|Nozuchi|野槌|}} | script_name = Japanese | script = 草祖草野 | parents = [[Izanami]] and [[Izanagi]] | siblings = [[Ōyamatsumi]] | offspring = | gender = Female }}
{{Nihongo|'''Kaya-no-hime'''|草祖草野|}}, also called {{Nihongo|'''Kanoyanohime-no-Kami'''|鹿屋野比売神|}}, {{Nihongo|'''Kusanooyakaya-no-hime'''|草祖草野姫|}}, {{Nihongo|'''Kusano-hime'''|草野姫命|}}, or {{Nihongo|'''Nozuchi'''|野槌|}} (meaning Moorland Elder),<ref>{{Cite web|title=Kayanohime • A History of Japan - 日本歴史|url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/kayanohime/|access-date=2020-10-14|website=A History of Japan - 日本歴史|language=en-GB}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&q=Nozuchi-no-kami&pg=PA501|title=Japan Encyclopedia|author=Louis-Frédéric|date=2005|publisher=Harvard University Press|isbn=978-0-674-01753-5|language=en}}</ref> is the Japanese goddess of vegetation, grass, and fields. She is considered protector of fields.<ref> {{Cite book|last1=Picken|first1=Stuart D. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=yA3_11C6pPgC&dq=Kaya-no-hime&pg=PA99|title=Essentials of Shinto: An Analytical Guide to Principal Teachings|last2=Picken|first2=Stuart|date=1994|publisher=Greenwood Publishing Group|isbn=978-0-313-26431-3|language=en}} </ref><ref>{{Cite book|last1=Buttimer|first1=Anne|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=zUHFyGQcJxgC&q=Nozuchi-no-kami&pg=PA51|title=Nature and Identity in Cross-Cultural Perspective|last2=Wallin|first2=L.|date=1999-07-31|publisher=Springer Science & Business Media|isbn=978-0-7923-5651-6|language=en}}</ref> She is also considered the ancestor of [[herb]]s.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Aveni|first=Anthony|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=ueolEAAAQBAJ&dq=Kaya-no-hime&pg=PA144|title=Creation Stories: Landscapes and the Human Imagination|date=2021-04-20|publisher=Yale University Press|isbn=978-0-300-25124-1|pages=144|language=en|author-link=Anthony Aveni}}</ref>
She is the daughter of [[Izanami]] and [[Izanagi]]. She is married to her husband and brother [[Ōyamatsumi]], and gave birth to eight deities. Some versions of her origin story claim she is also the mother of [[Konohanasakuya-hime]].<ref> {{Cite book|last1=Coulter|first1=Charles Russell|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=VWxekbhM1yEC&dq=Kaya-no-hime&pg=PT604|title=Encyclopedia of Ancient Deities|last2=Turner|first2=Patricia|date=2013-07-04|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-135-96397-2|language=en}} </ref>
== Worship == In the past, people often prayed to her before cutting down wood or reeds for building.<ref>{{Cite book|last1=Hastings|first1=James|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=nZ5DAQAAMAAJ&q=Kaya-no-hime&pg=PA467|title=Encyclopaedia of Religion and Ethics: Sacrifice-Sudra|last2=Selbie|first2=John Alexander|date=1921|publisher=T. & T. Clark|isbn=978-0-567-06509-4|language=en}}</ref> She was worshipped because the plants that she favored provided the raw materials for furniture and houses.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Aston|first=W. G.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=FT6yDwAAQBAJ&dq=Kaya-no-hime&pg=PA120|title=Shinto|date=2019-09-25|publisher=BoD – Books on Demand|isbn=978-3-7340-7989-4|pages=120|language=en}}</ref>

{{Nihongo|'''Kaya-no-hime'''|草祖草野|}}, also called {{Nihongo|'''Kanoyanohime-no-Kami'''|鹿屋野比売神|}}, {{Nihongo|'''Kusanooyakaya-no-hime'''|草祖草野姫|}}, {{Nihongo|'''Kusano-hime'''|草野姫命|}}, or {{Nihongo|'''Nozuchi'''|野槌|}} (meaning Moorland Elder),<ref>{{Cite web|title=Kayanohime • A History of Japan - 日本歴史|url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/kayanohime/|access-date=2020-10-14|website=A History of Japan - 日本歴史|language=en-GB}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&q=Nozuchi-no-kami&pg=PA501|title=Japan Encyclopedia|author=Louis-Frédéric|date=2005|publisher=Harvard University Press|isbn=978-0-674-01753-5|language=en}}</ref> is the Japanese goddess of vegetation, grass, and fields. She is considered protector of fields.<ref> {{Cite book|last1=Picken|first1=Stuart D. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=yA3_11C6pPgC&dq=Kaya-no-hime&pg=PA99|title=Essentials of Shinto: An Analytical Guide to Principal Teachings|last2=Picken|first2=Stuart|date=1994|publisher=Greenwood Publishing Group|isbn=978-0-313-26431-3|language=en}} </ref><ref>{{Cite book|last1=Buttimer|first1=Anne|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=zUHFyGQcJxgC&q=Nozuchi-no-kami&pg=PA51|title=Nature and Identity in Cross-Cultural Perspective|last2=Wallin|first2=L.|date=1999-07-31|publisher=Springer Science & Business Media|isbn=978-0-7923-5651-6|language=en}}</ref> She is also considered the ancestor of [[:en:Herb|Herb]]s.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Aveni|first=Anthony|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=ueolEAAAQBAJ&dq=Kaya-no-hime&pg=PA144|title=Creation Stories: Landscapes and the Human Imagination|date=2021-04-20|publisher=Yale University Press|isbn=978-0-300-25124-1|pages=144|language=en|author-link=Anthony Aveni}}</ref>
She is the daughter of [[:en:Izanami|Izanami]] and [[Izanagi]]. She is married to her husband and brother [[Ōyamatsumi]], and gave birth to eight deities. Some versions of her origin story claim she is also the mother of [[Konohanasakuya-hime]].<ref> {{Cite book|last1=Coulter|first1=Charles Russell|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=VWxekbhM1yEC&dq=Kaya-no-hime&pg=PT604|title=Encyclopedia of Ancient Deities|last2=Turner|first2=Patricia|date=2013-07-04|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-135-96397-2|language=en}} </ref>
== Worship == In the past, people often prayed to her before cutting down wood or reeds for building.<ref>{{Cite book|last1=Hastings|first1=James|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=nZ5DAQAAMAAJ&q=Kaya-no-hime&pg=PA467|title=Encyclopaedia of Religion and Ethics: Sacrifice-Sudra|last2=Selbie|first2=John Alexander|date=1921|publisher=T. & T. Clark|isbn=978-0-567-06509-4|language=en}}</ref> She was worshipped because the plants that she favored provided the raw materials for furniture and houses.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Aston|first=W. G.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=FT6yDwAAQBAJ&dq=Kaya-no-hime&pg=PA120|title=Shinto|date=2019-09-25|publisher=BoD – Books on Demand|isbn=978-3-7340-7989-4|pages=120|language=en}}</ref>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kaya-no-hime]][[es:Kaya-no-hime]][[fa:کایا-نو-هیمه]][[ja:カヤノヒメ]][[zh:鹿屋野比賣神]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q8191878]] Wikidata name: Kaya-no-hime [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:28, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q8191878]] Wikidata name: Kaya-no-hime [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:07, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Shinto kami}} {{Expand language|topic=|langcode=ja|otherarticle=カヤノヒメ|date=October 2022}} {{Page numbers needed|date=August 2021}} {{Infobox deity | type = Japanese | name = Kaya-no-hime | deity_of = Goddess of vegetation, grass and fields | member_of = | image = | caption = | other_names = {{Nihongo|Kanoyanohime-no-Kami|鹿屋野比売神|}}, {{Nihongo|Kusanooyakaya-no-hime|草祖草野姫|}}, {{Nihongo|Kusano-hime|草野姫命|}}, and {{Nihongo|Nozuchi|野槌|}} | script_name = Japanese | script = 草祖草野 | parents =[[:en:Izanami|Izanami]] and [[Izanagi]]; {{ill|Izanagi|WD=Q813858}}; {{ill|Izanami|WD=Q682306}}| siblings = [[Ōyamatsumi]] | offspring = | gender =Female; {{ill|female|WD=Q6581072}}| consort= {{ill|Ōyamatsumi|WD=Q386563}} }}
{{Nihongo|'''Kaya-no-hime'''|草祖草野|}}, also called {{Nihongo|'''Kanoyanohime-no-Kami'''|鹿屋野比売神|}}, {{Nihongo|'''Kusanooyakaya-no-hime'''|草祖草野姫|}}, {{Nihongo|'''Kusano-hime'''|草野姫命|}}, or {{Nihongo|'''Nozuchi'''|野槌|}} (meaning Moorland Elder),<ref>{{Cite web|title=Kayanohime • A History of Japan - 日本歴史|url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/kayanohime/|access-date=2020-10-14|website=A History of Japan - 日本歴史|language=en-GB}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&q=Nozuchi-no-kami&pg=PA501|title=Japan Encyclopedia|author=Louis-Frédéric|date=2005|publisher=Harvard University Press|isbn=978-0-674-01753-5|language=en}}</ref> is the Japanese goddess of vegetation, grass, and fields. She is considered protector of fields.<ref> {{Cite book|last1=Picken|first1=Stuart D. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=yA3_11C6pPgC&dq=Kaya-no-hime&pg=PA99|title=Essentials of Shinto: An Analytical Guide to Principal Teachings|last2=Picken|first2=Stuart|date=1994|publisher=Greenwood Publishing Group|isbn=978-0-313-26431-3|language=en}} </ref><ref>{{Cite book|last1=Buttimer|first1=Anne|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=zUHFyGQcJxgC&q=Nozuchi-no-kami&pg=PA51|title=Nature and Identity in Cross-Cultural Perspective|last2=Wallin|first2=L.|date=1999-07-31|publisher=Springer Science & Business Media|isbn=978-0-7923-5651-6|language=en}}</ref> She is also considered the ancestor of [[:en:Herb|Herb]]s.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Aveni|first=Anthony|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=ueolEAAAQBAJ&dq=Kaya-no-hime&pg=PA144|title=Creation Stories: Landscapes and the Human Imagination|date=2021-04-20|publisher=Yale University Press|isbn=978-0-300-25124-1|pages=144|language=en|author-link=Anthony Aveni}}</ref>
She is the daughter of [[:en:Izanami|Izanami]] and [[Izanagi]]. She is married to her husband and brother [[Ōyamatsumi]], and gave birth to eight deities. Some versions of her origin story claim she is also the mother of [[Konohanasakuya-hime]].<ref> {{Cite book|last1=Coulter|first1=Charles Russell|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=VWxekbhM1yEC&dq=Kaya-no-hime&pg=PT604|title=Encyclopedia of Ancient Deities|last2=Turner|first2=Patricia|date=2013-07-04|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-135-96397-2|language=en}} </ref>
== Worship == In the past, people often prayed to her before cutting down wood or reeds for building.<ref>{{Cite book|last1=Hastings|first1=James|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=nZ5DAQAAMAAJ&q=Kaya-no-hime&pg=PA467|title=Encyclopaedia of Religion and Ethics: Sacrifice-Sudra|last2=Selbie|first2=John Alexander|date=1921|publisher=T. & T. Clark|isbn=978-0-567-06509-4|language=en}}</ref> She was worshipped because the plants that she favored provided the raw materials for furniture and houses.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Aston|first=W. G.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=FT6yDwAAQBAJ&dq=Kaya-no-hime&pg=PA120|title=Shinto|date=2019-09-25|publisher=BoD – Books on Demand|isbn=978-3-7340-7989-4|pages=120|language=en}}</ref>

She is considered protector of fields.<ref> {{Cite book|last1=Picken|first1=Stuart D. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=yA3_11C6pPgC&dq=Kaya-no-hime&pg=PA99|title=Essentials of Shinto: An Analytical Guide to Principal Teachings|last2=Picken|first2=Stuart|date=1994|publisher=Greenwood Publishing Group|isbn=978-0-313-26431-3|language=en}} </ref><ref>{{Cite book|last1=Buttimer|first1=Anne|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=zUHFyGQcJxgC&q=Nozuchi-no-kami&pg=PA51|title=Nature and Identity in Cross-Cultural Perspective|last2=Wallin|first2=L.|date=1999-07-31|publisher=Springer Science & Business Media|isbn=978-0-7923-5651-6|language=en}}</ref> She is also considered the ancestor of [[:en:Herb|Herb]]s.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Aveni|first=Anthony|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=ueolEAAAQBAJ&dq=Kaya-no-hime&pg=PA144|title=Creation Stories: Landscapes and the Human Imagination|date=2021-04-20|publisher=Yale University Press|isbn=978-0-300-25124-1|pages=144|language=en|author-link=Anthony Aveni}}</ref>
She is the daughter of [[:en:Izanami|Izanami]] and [[Izanagi]]. She is married to her husband and brother [[Ōyamatsumi]], and gave birth to eight deities. Some versions of her origin story claim she is also the mother of [[Konohanasakuya-hime]].<ref> {{Cite book|last1=Coulter|first1=Charles Russell|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=VWxekbhM1yEC&dq=Kaya-no-hime&pg=PT604|title=Encyclopedia of Ancient Deities|last2=Turner|first2=Patricia|date=2013-07-04|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-135-96397-2|language=en}} </ref>
== Worship == In the past, people often prayed to her before cutting down wood or reeds for building.<ref>{{Cite book|last1=Hastings|first1=James|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=nZ5DAQAAMAAJ&q=Kaya-no-hime&pg=PA467|title=Encyclopaedia of Religion and Ethics: Sacrifice-Sudra|last2=Selbie|first2=John Alexander|date=1921|publisher=T. & T. Clark|isbn=978-0-567-06509-4|language=en}}</ref> She was worshipped because the plants that she favored provided the raw materials for furniture and houses.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Aston|first=W. G.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=FT6yDwAAQBAJ&dq=Kaya-no-hime&pg=PA120|title=Shinto|date=2019-09-25|publisher=BoD – Books on Demand|isbn=978-3-7340-7989-4|pages=120|language=en}}</ref>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kaya-no-hime]][[es:Kaya-no-hime]][[fa:کایا-نو-هیمه]][[ja:カヤノヒメ]][[zh:鹿屋野比賣神]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kaya-no-hime]][[es:Kaya-no-hime]][[fa:کایا-نو-هیمه]][[ja:カヤノヒメ]][[zh:鹿屋野比賣神]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kaya-no-hime]][[es:Kaya-no-hime]][[fa:کایا-نو-هیمه]][[ja:カヤノヒメ]][[zh:鹿屋野比賣神]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q8191878]] Wikidata name: Kaya-no-hime [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:28, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q8191878]] Wikidata name: Kaya-no-hime [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:07, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Shinto kami}} {{Expand language|topic=|langcode=ja|otherarticle=カヤノヒメ|date=October 2022}} {{Page numbers needed|date=August 2021}} {{Infobox deity | type = Japanese | name = Kaya-no-hime | deity_of = Goddess of vegetation, grass and fields | member_of = | image = | caption = | other_names = {{Nihongo|Kanoyanohime-no-Kami|鹿屋野比売神|}}, {{Nihongo|Kusanooyakaya-no-hime|草祖草野姫|}}, {{Nihongo|Kusano-hime|草野姫命|}}, and {{Nihongo|Nozuchi|野槌|}} | script_name = Japanese | script = 草祖草野 | parents =[[:en:Izanami|Izanami]] and [[Izanagi]]; {{ill|Izanagi|WD=Q813858}}; {{ill|Izanami|WD=Q682306}}| siblings = [[Ōyamatsumi]] | offspring = | gender =Female; {{ill|female|WD=Q6581072}}| consort= {{ill|Ōyamatsumi|WD=Q386563}} }}
{{Nihongo|'''Kaya-no-hime'''|草祖草野|}}, also called {{Nihongo|'''Kanoyanohime-no-Kami'''|鹿屋野比売神|}}, {{Nihongo|'''Kusanooyakaya-no-hime'''|草祖草野姫|}}, {{Nihongo|'''Kusano-hime'''|草野姫命|}}, or {{Nihongo|'''Nozuchi'''|野槌|}} (meaning Moorland Elder),<ref>{{Cite web|title=Kayanohime • A History of Japan - 日本歴史|url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/kayanohime/|access-date=2020-10-14|website=A History of Japan - 日本歴史|language=en-GB}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&q=Nozuchi-no-kami&pg=PA501|title=Japan Encyclopedia|author=Louis-Frédéric|date=2005|publisher=Harvard University Press|isbn=978-0-674-01753-5|language=en}}</ref> is the Japanese goddess of vegetation, grass, and fields. She is considered protector of fields.<ref> {{Cite book|last1=Picken|first1=Stuart D. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=yA3_11C6pPgC&dq=Kaya-no-hime&pg=PA99|title=Essentials of Shinto: An Analytical Guide to Principal Teachings|last2=Picken|first2=Stuart|date=1994|publisher=Greenwood Publishing Group|isbn=978-0-313-26431-3|language=en}} </ref><ref>{{Cite book|last1=Buttimer|first1=Anne|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=zUHFyGQcJxgC&q=Nozuchi-no-kami&pg=PA51|title=Nature and Identity in Cross-Cultural Perspective|last2=Wallin|first2=L.|date=1999-07-31|publisher=Springer Science & Business Media|isbn=978-0-7923-5651-6|language=en}}</ref> She is also considered the ancestor of [[:en:Herb|Herb]]s.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Aveni|first=Anthony|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=ueolEAAAQBAJ&dq=Kaya-no-hime&pg=PA144|title=Creation Stories: Landscapes and the Human Imagination|date=2021-04-20|publisher=Yale University Press|isbn=978-0-300-25124-1|pages=144|language=en|author-link=Anthony Aveni}}</ref>
She is the daughter of [[:en:Izanami|Izanami]] and [[Izanagi]]. She is married to her husband and brother [[Ōyamatsumi]], and gave birth to eight deities. Some versions of her origin story claim she is also the mother of [[Konohanasakuya-hime]].<ref> {{Cite book|last1=Coulter|first1=Charles Russell|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=VWxekbhM1yEC&dq=Kaya-no-hime&pg=PT604|title=Encyclopedia of Ancient Deities|last2=Turner|first2=Patricia|date=2013-07-04|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-135-96397-2|language=en}} </ref>
== Worship == In the past, people often prayed to her before cutting down wood or reeds for building.<ref>{{Cite book|last1=Hastings|first1=James|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=nZ5DAQAAMAAJ&q=Kaya-no-hime&pg=PA467|title=Encyclopaedia of Religion and Ethics: Sacrifice-Sudra|last2=Selbie|first2=John Alexander|date=1921|publisher=T. & T. Clark|isbn=978-0-567-06509-4|language=en}}</ref> She was worshipped because the plants that she favored provided the raw materials for furniture and houses.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Aston|first=W. G.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=FT6yDwAAQBAJ&dq=Kaya-no-hime&pg=PA120|title=Shinto|date=2019-09-25|publisher=BoD – Books on Demand|isbn=978-3-7340-7989-4|pages=120|language=en}}</ref>

She is considered protector of fields.<ref> {{Cite book|last1=Picken|first1=Stuart D. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=yA3_11C6pPgC&dq=Kaya-no-hime&pg=PA99|title=Essentials of Shinto: An Analytical Guide to Principal Teachings|last2=Picken|first2=Stuart|date=1994|publisher=Greenwood Publishing Group|isbn=978-0-313-26431-3|language=en}} </ref><ref>{{Cite book|last1=Buttimer|first1=Anne|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=zUHFyGQcJxgC&q=Nozuchi-no-kami&pg=PA51|title=Nature and Identity in Cross-Cultural Perspective|last2=Wallin|first2=L.|date=1999-07-31|publisher=Springer Science & Business Media|isbn=978-0-7923-5651-6|language=en}}</ref> She is also considered the ancestor of [[:en:Herb|Herb]]s.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Aveni|first=Anthony|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=ueolEAAAQBAJ&dq=Kaya-no-hime&pg=PA144|title=Creation Stories: Landscapes and the Human Imagination|date=2021-04-20|publisher=Yale University Press|isbn=978-0-300-25124-1|pages=144|language=en|author-link=Anthony Aveni}}</ref>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kaya-no-hime]][[es:Kaya-no-hime]][[fa:کایا-نو-هیمه]][[ja:カヤノヒメ]][[zh:鹿屋野比賣神]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kaya-no-hime]][[es:Kaya-no-hime]][[fa:کایا-نو-هیمه]][[ja:カヤノヒメ]][[zh:鹿屋野比賣神]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kaya-no-hime]][[es:Kaya-no-hime]][[fa:کایا-نو-هیمه]][[ja:カヤノヒメ]][[zh:鹿屋野比賣神]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q8191878]] Wikidata name: Kaya-no-hime [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:28, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q8191878]] Wikidata name: Kaya-no-hime [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:07, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Shinto kami}} {{Expand language|topic=|langcode=ja|otherarticle=カヤノヒメ|date=October 2022}} {{Page numbers needed|date=August 2021}} {{Infobox deity | type = Japanese | name = Kaya-no-hime | deity_of = Goddess of vegetation, grass and fields | member_of = | image = | caption = | other_names = {{Nihongo|Kanoyanohime-no-Kami|鹿屋野比売神|}}, {{Nihongo|Kusanooyakaya-no-hime|草祖草野姫|}}, {{Nihongo|Kusano-hime|草野姫命|}}, and {{Nihongo|Nozuchi|野槌|}} | script_name = Japanese | script = 草祖草野 | parents =[[:en:Izanami|Izanami]] and [[Izanagi]]; {{ill|Izanagi|WD=Q813858}}; {{ill|Izanami|WD=Q682306}}| siblings = [[Ōyamatsumi]] | offspring = | gender =Female; {{ill|female|WD=Q6581072}}| consort= {{ill|Ōyamatsumi|WD=Q386563}} }}
{{Nihongo|'''Kaya-no-hime'''|草祖草野|}}, also called {{Nihongo|'''Kanoyanohime-no-Kami'''|鹿屋野比売神|}}, {{Nihongo|'''Kusanooyakaya-no-hime'''|草祖草野姫|}}, {{Nihongo|'''Kusano-hime'''|草野姫命|}}, or {{Nihongo|'''Nozuchi'''|野槌|}} (meaning Moorland Elder),<ref>{{Cite web|title=Kayanohime • A History of Japan - 日本歴史|url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/kayanohime/|access-date=2020-10-14|website=A History of Japan - 日本歴史|language=en-GB}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&q=Nozuchi-no-kami&pg=PA501|title=Japan Encyclopedia|author=Louis-Frédéric|date=2005|publisher=Harvard University Press|isbn=978-0-674-01753-5|language=en}}</ref> is the Japanese goddess of vegetation, grass, and fields. She is considered protector of fields.<ref> {{Cite book|last1=Picken|first1=Stuart D. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=yA3_11C6pPgC&dq=Kaya-no-hime&pg=PA99|title=Essentials of Shinto: An Analytical Guide to Principal Teachings|last2=Picken|first2=Stuart|date=1994|publisher=Greenwood Publishing Group|isbn=978-0-313-26431-3|language=en}} </ref><ref>{{Cite book|last1=Buttimer|first1=Anne|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=zUHFyGQcJxgC&q=Nozuchi-no-kami&pg=PA51|title=Nature and Identity in Cross-Cultural Perspective|last2=Wallin|first2=L.|date=1999-07-31|publisher=Springer Science & Business Media|isbn=978-0-7923-5651-6|language=en}}</ref> She is also considered the ancestor of [[:en:Herb|Herb]]s.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Aveni|first=Anthony|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=ueolEAAAQBAJ&dq=Kaya-no-hime&pg=PA144|title=Creation Stories: Landscapes and the Human Imagination|date=2021-04-20|publisher=Yale University Press|isbn=978-0-300-25124-1|pages=144|language=en|author-link=Anthony Aveni}}</ref>
She is the daughter of [[:en:Izanami|Izanami]] and [[Izanagi]]. She is married to her husband and brother [[Ōyamatsumi]], and gave birth to eight deities. Some versions of her origin story claim she is also the mother of [[Konohanasakuya-hime]].<ref> {{Cite book|last1=Coulter|first1=Charles Russell|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=VWxekbhM1yEC&dq=Kaya-no-hime&pg=PT604|title=Encyclopedia of Ancient Deities|last2=Turner|first2=Patricia|date=2013-07-04|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-135-96397-2|language=en}} </ref>
== Worship == In the past, people often prayed to her before cutting down wood or reeds for building.<ref>{{Cite book|last1=Hastings|first1=James|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=nZ5DAQAAMAAJ&q=Kaya-no-hime&pg=PA467|title=Encyclopaedia of Religion and Ethics: Sacrifice-Sudra|last2=Selbie|first2=John Alexander|date=1921|publisher=T. & T. Clark|isbn=978-0-567-06509-4|language=en}}</ref> She was worshipped because the plants that she favored provided the raw materials for furniture and houses.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Aston|first=W. G.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=FT6yDwAAQBAJ&dq=Kaya-no-hime&pg=PA120|title=Shinto|date=2019-09-25|publisher=BoD – Books on Demand|isbn=978-3-7340-7989-4|pages=120|language=en}}</ref>

{{Nihongo|'''Kaya-no-hime'''|草祖草野|}}, also called {{Nihongo|'''Kanoyanohime-no-Kami'''|鹿屋野比売神|}}, {{Nihongo|'''Kusanooyakaya-no-hime'''|草祖草野姫|}}, {{Nihongo|'''Kusano-hime'''|草野姫命|}}, or {{Nihongo|'''Nozuchi'''|野槌|}} (meaning Moorland Elder),<ref>{{Cite web|title=Kayanohime • A History of Japan - 日本歴史|url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/kayanohime/|access-date=2020-10-14|website=A History of Japan - 日本歴史|language=en-GB}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&q=Nozuchi-no-kami&pg=PA501|title=Japan Encyclopedia|author=Louis-Frédéric|date=2005|publisher=Harvard University Press|isbn=978-0-674-01753-5|language=en}}</ref> is the Japanese goddess of vegetation, grass, and fields. She is considered protector of fields.<ref> {{Cite book|last1=Picken|first1=Stuart D. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=yA3_11C6pPgC&dq=Kaya-no-hime&pg=PA99|title=Essentials of Shinto: An Analytical Guide to Principal Teachings|last2=Picken|first2=Stuart|date=1994|publisher=Greenwood Publishing Group|isbn=978-0-313-26431-3|language=en}} </ref><ref>{{Cite book|last1=Buttimer|first1=Anne|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=zUHFyGQcJxgC&q=Nozuchi-no-kami&pg=PA51|title=Nature and Identity in Cross-Cultural Perspective|last2=Wallin|first2=L.|date=1999-07-31|publisher=Springer Science & Business Media|isbn=978-0-7923-5651-6|language=en}}</ref> She is also considered the ancestor of {{ill|Herb|en|Herb|WD=Q207123|lt=Herb}}s.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Aveni|first=Anthony|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=ueolEAAAQBAJ&dq=Kaya-no-hime&pg=PA144|title=Creation Stories: Landscapes and the Human Imagination|date=2021-04-20|publisher=Yale University Press|isbn=978-0-300-25124-1|pages=144|language=en|author-link=Anthony Aveni}}</ref>
She is the daughter of {{ill|Izanami|en|Izanami|WD=Q682306|lt=Izanami}} and [[Izanagi]]. She is married to her husband and brother [[Ōyamatsumi]], and gave birth to eight deities. Some versions of her origin story claim she is also the mother of [[Konohanasakuya-hime]].<ref> {{Cite book|last1=Coulter|first1=Charles Russell|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=VWxekbhM1yEC&dq=Kaya-no-hime&pg=PT604|title=Encyclopedia of Ancient Deities|last2=Turner|first2=Patricia|date=2013-07-04|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-135-96397-2|language=en}} </ref>
== Worship == In the past, people often prayed to her before cutting down wood or reeds for building.<ref>{{Cite book|last1=Hastings|first1=James|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=nZ5DAQAAMAAJ&q=Kaya-no-hime&pg=PA467|title=Encyclopaedia of Religion and Ethics: Sacrifice-Sudra|last2=Selbie|first2=John Alexander|date=1921|publisher=T. & T. Clark|isbn=978-0-567-06509-4|language=en}}</ref> She was worshipped because the plants that she favored provided the raw materials for furniture and houses.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Aston|first=W. G.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=FT6yDwAAQBAJ&dq=Kaya-no-hime&pg=PA120|title=Shinto|date=2019-09-25|publisher=BoD – Books on Demand|isbn=978-3-7340-7989-4|pages=120|language=en}}</ref>

{{Nihongo|'''Kaya-no-hime'''|草祖草野|}}, also called {{Nihongo|'''Kanoyanohime-no-Kami'''|鹿屋野比売神|}}, {{Nihongo|'''Kusanooyakaya-no-hime'''|草祖草野姫|}}, {{Nihongo|'''Kusano-hime'''|草野姫命|}}, or {{Nihongo|'''Nozuchi'''|野槌|}} (meaning Moorland Elder),<ref>{{Cite web|title=Kayanohime • A History of Japan - 日本歴史|url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/kayanohime/|access-date=2020-10-14|website=A History of Japan - 日本歴史|language=en-GB}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&q=Nozuchi-no-kami&pg=PA501|title=Japan Encyclopedia|author=Louis-Frédéric|date=2005|publisher=Harvard University Press|isbn=978-0-674-01753-5|language=en}}</ref> is the Japanese goddess of vegetation, grass, and fields. She is considered protector of fields.<ref> {{Cite book|last1=Picken|first1=Stuart D. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=yA3_11C6pPgC&dq=Kaya-no-hime&pg=PA99|title=Essentials of Shinto: An Analytical Guide to Principal Teachings|last2=Picken|first2=Stuart|date=1994|publisher=Greenwood Publishing Group|isbn=978-0-313-26431-3|language=en}} </ref><ref>{{Cite book|last1=Buttimer|first1=Anne|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=zUHFyGQcJxgC&q=Nozuchi-no-kami&pg=PA51|title=Nature and Identity in Cross-Cultural Perspective|last2=Wallin|first2=L.|date=1999-07-31|publisher=Springer Science & Business Media|isbn=978-0-7923-5651-6|language=en}}</ref> She is also considered the ancestor of {{ill|Herb|en|Herb|WD=Q207123|lt=Herb}}s.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Aveni|first=Anthony|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=ueolEAAAQBAJ&dq=Kaya-no-hime&pg=PA144|title=Creation Stories: Landscapes and the Human Imagination|date=2021-04-20|publisher=Yale University Press|isbn=978-0-300-25124-1|pages=144|language=en|author-link=Anthony Aveni}}</ref>
She is the daughter of {{ill|Izanami|en|Izanami|WD=Q682306|lt=Izanami}} and [[Izanagi]]. She is married to her husband and brother [[Ōyamatsumi]], and gave birth to eight deities. Some versions of her origin story claim she is also the mother of [[Konohanasakuya-hime]].<ref> {{Cite book|last1=Coulter|first1=Charles Russell|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=VWxekbhM1yEC&dq=Kaya-no-hime&pg=PT604|title=Encyclopedia of Ancient Deities|last2=Turner|first2=Patricia|date=2013-07-04|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-135-96397-2|language=en}} </ref>
== Worship == In the past, people often prayed to her before cutting down wood or reeds for building.<ref>{{Cite book|last1=Hastings|first1=James|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=nZ5DAQAAMAAJ&q=Kaya-no-hime&pg=PA467|title=Encyclopaedia of Religion and Ethics: Sacrifice-Sudra|last2=Selbie|first2=John Alexander|date=1921|publisher=T. & T. Clark|isbn=978-0-567-06509-4|language=en}}</ref> She was worshipped because the plants that she favored provided the raw materials for furniture and houses.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Aston|first=W. G.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=FT6yDwAAQBAJ&dq=Kaya-no-hime&pg=PA120|title=Shinto|date=2019-09-25|publisher=BoD – Books on Demand|isbn=978-3-7340-7989-4|pages=120|language=en}}</ref>

{{Nihongo|'''Kaya-no-hime'''|草祖草野|}}, also called {{Nihongo|'''Kanoyanohime-no-Kami'''|鹿屋野比売神|}}, {{Nihongo|'''Kusanooyakaya-no-hime'''|草祖草野姫|}}, {{Nihongo|'''Kusano-hime'''|草野姫命|}}, or {{Nihongo|'''Nozuchi'''|野槌|}} (meaning Moorland Elder),<ref>{{Cite web|title=Kayanohime • A History of Japan - 日本歴史|url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/kayanohime/|access-date=2020-10-14|website=A History of Japan - 日本歴史|language=en-GB}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&q=Nozuchi-no-kami&pg=PA501|title=Japan Encyclopedia|author=Louis-Frédéric|date=2005|publisher=Harvard University Press|isbn=978-0-674-01753-5|language=en}}</ref> is the Japanese goddess of vegetation, grass, and fields. She is considered protector of fields.<ref> {{Cite book|last1=Picken|first1=Stuart D. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=yA3_11C6pPgC&dq=Kaya-no-hime&pg=PA99|title=Essentials of Shinto: An Analytical Guide to Principal Teachings|last2=Picken|first2=Stuart|date=1994|publisher=Greenwood Publishing Group|isbn=978-0-313-26431-3|language=en}} </ref><ref>{{Cite book|last1=Buttimer|first1=Anne|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=zUHFyGQcJxgC&q=Nozuchi-no-kami&pg=PA51|title=Nature and Identity in Cross-Cultural Perspective|last2=Wallin|first2=L.|date=1999-07-31|publisher=Springer Science & Business Media|isbn=978-0-7923-5651-6|language=en}}</ref> She is also considered the ancestor of {{ill|Herb|en|Herb|WD=Q207123|lt=Herb}}s.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Aveni|first=Anthony|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=ueolEAAAQBAJ&dq=Kaya-no-hime&pg=PA144|title=Creation Stories: Landscapes and the Human Imagination|date=2021-04-20|publisher=Yale University Press|isbn=978-0-300-25124-1|pages=144|language=en|author-link=Anthony Aveni}}</ref>
She is the daughter of {{ill|Izanami|en|Izanami|WD=Q682306|lt=Izanami}} and [[Izanagi]]. She is married to her husband and brother [[Ōyamatsumi]], and gave birth to eight deities. Some versions of her origin story claim she is also the mother of [[Konohanasakuya-hime]].<ref> {{Cite book|last1=Coulter|first1=Charles Russell|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=VWxekbhM1yEC&dq=Kaya-no-hime&pg=PT604|title=Encyclopedia of Ancient Deities|last2=Turner|first2=Patricia|date=2013-07-04|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-135-96397-2|language=en}} </ref>
== Worship == In the past, people often prayed to her before cutting down wood or reeds for building.<ref>{{Cite book|last1=Hastings|first1=James|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=nZ5DAQAAMAAJ&q=Kaya-no-hime&pg=PA467|title=Encyclopaedia of Religion and Ethics: Sacrifice-Sudra|last2=Selbie|first2=John Alexander|date=1921|publisher=T. & T. Clark|isbn=978-0-567-06509-4|language=en}}</ref> She was worshipped because the plants that she favored provided the raw materials for furniture and houses.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Aston|first=W. G.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=FT6yDwAAQBAJ&dq=Kaya-no-hime&pg=PA120|title=Shinto|date=2019-09-25|publisher=BoD – Books on Demand|isbn=978-3-7340-7989-4|pages=120|language=en}}</ref>

She is the daughter of {{ill|Izanami|en|Izanami|WD=Q682306|lt=Izanami|1=Izanami}} and [[Izanagi]]. She is married to her husband and brother [[Ōyamatsumi]], and gave birth to eight deities. Some versions of her origin story claim she is also the mother of [[Konohanasakuya-hime]].<ref> {{Cite book|last1=Coulter|first1=Charles Russell|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=VWxekbhM1yEC&dq=Kaya-no-hime&pg=PT604|title=Encyclopedia of Ancient Deities|last2=Turner|first2=Patricia|date=2013-07-04|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-135-96397-2|language=en}} </ref>
== Worship == In the past, people often prayed to her before cutting down wood or reeds for building.<ref>{{Cite book|last1=Hastings|first1=James|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=nZ5DAQAAMAAJ&q=Kaya-no-hime&pg=PA467|title=Encyclopaedia of Religion and Ethics: Sacrifice-Sudra|last2=Selbie|first2=John Alexander|date=1921|publisher=T. & T. Clark|isbn=978-0-567-06509-4|language=en}}</ref> She was worshipped because the plants that she favored provided the raw materials for furniture and houses.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Aston|first=W. G.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=FT6yDwAAQBAJ&dq=Kaya-no-hime&pg=PA120|title=Shinto|date=2019-09-25|publisher=BoD – Books on Demand|isbn=978-3-7340-7989-4|pages=120|language=en}}</ref>

{{Nihongo|'''Kaya-no-hime'''|草祖草野|}}, also called {{Nihongo|'''Kanoyanohime-no-Kami'''|鹿屋野比売神|}}, {{Nihongo|'''Kusanooyakaya-no-hime'''|草祖草野姫|}}, {{Nihongo|'''Kusano-hime'''|草野姫命|}}, or {{Nihongo|'''Nozuchi'''|野槌|}} (meaning Moorland Elder),<ref>{{Cite web|title=Kayanohime • A History of Japan - 日本歴史|url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/kayanohime/|access-date=2020-10-14|website=A History of Japan - 日本歴史|language=en-GB}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&q=Nozuchi-no-kami&pg=PA501|title=Japan Encyclopedia|author=Louis-Frédéric|date=2005|publisher=Harvard University Press|isbn=978-0-674-01753-5|language=en}}</ref> is the Japanese goddess of vegetation, grass, and fields. She is considered protector of fields.<ref> {{Cite book|last1=Picken|first1=Stuart D. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=yA3_11C6pPgC&dq=Kaya-no-hime&pg=PA99|title=Essentials of Shinto: An Analytical Guide to Principal Teachings|last2=Picken|first2=Stuart|date=1994|publisher=Greenwood Publishing Group|isbn=978-0-313-26431-3|language=en}} </ref><ref>{{Cite book|last1=Buttimer|first1=Anne|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=zUHFyGQcJxgC&q=Nozuchi-no-kami&pg=PA51|title=Nature and Identity in Cross-Cultural Perspective|last2=Wallin|first2=L.|date=1999-07-31|publisher=Springer Science & Business Media|isbn=978-0-7923-5651-6|language=en}}</ref> She is also considered the ancestor of {{ill|Herb|en|Herb|WD=Q207123|lt=Herb|1=Herb}}s.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Aveni|first=Anthony|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=ueolEAAAQBAJ&dq=Kaya-no-hime&pg=PA144|title=Creation Stories: Landscapes and the Human Imagination|date=2021-04-20|publisher=Yale University Press|isbn=978-0-300-25124-1|pages=144|language=en|author-link=Anthony Aveni}}</ref>
She is the daughter of {{ill|Izanami|en|Izanami|WD=Q682306|lt=Izanami|1=Izanami}} and [[Izanagi]]. She is married to her husband and brother [[Ōyamatsumi]], and gave birth to eight deities. Some versions of her origin story claim she is also the mother of [[Konohanasakuya-hime]].<ref> {{Cite book|last1=Coulter|first1=Charles Russell|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=VWxekbhM1yEC&dq=Kaya-no-hime&pg=PT604|title=Encyclopedia of Ancient Deities|last2=Turner|first2=Patricia|date=2013-07-04|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-135-96397-2|language=en}} </ref>
== Worship == In the past, people often prayed to her before cutting down wood or reeds for building.<ref>{{Cite book|last1=Hastings|first1=James|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=nZ5DAQAAMAAJ&q=Kaya-no-hime&pg=PA467|title=Encyclopaedia of Religion and Ethics: Sacrifice-Sudra|last2=Selbie|first2=John Alexander|date=1921|publisher=T. & T.
